title: Architect / Designer
audience: Enterprise, solution, security and systems architecture roles.

questions.text: When designing a system that uses AI, what is "technical debt" typically referring to?
options: Deferred licensing and platform costs created during AI implementation planning when planning enterprise AI architecture.
Design or implementation choices that are expedient in the short term but create future rework or quality issues.
Formal finance arrangements used to fund experimental AI research programs for reliable production AI delivery.
The training process used to tune model behaviour before production use when integrating AI services across platforms.

questions.text: What is the role of a "Proof of Concept" (PoC) in an AI project's design phase?
options: To release the complete AI solution to every production user immediately during solution design and governance review.
To demonstrate the feasibility and potential of a specific AI idea or technology on a small scale.
To produce the full operating documentation before design decisions are tested when integrating AI services across platforms.
To obtain full program funding before validating technical and business feasibility.

questions.text: In AI system design, what is "scalability"?
options: A measurement process for comparing the size of different deployed AI models for reliable production AI delivery.
The ability of a system to handle increasing workloads, users, or data volumes without significant performance loss.
A process for checking AI models and endpoints for security vulnerabilities when planning enterprise AI architecture.
The ease of creating visual screens and diagrams for an AI-enabled service during solution design and governance review.

questions.text: What is a "monolithic architecture" versus a "microservices architecture" in the context of AI application deployment?
options: Monolithic architectures are always preferred because AI services require one application when integrating AI services across platforms.
Monolithic means a single, tightly coupled application; microservices means breaking it into smaller, independent services.
Monolithic systems use earlier model types while microservices use newer model types during solution design and governance review.
Monolithic deployment is limited to on-premise systems while microservices require cloud for reliable production AI delivery.

questions.text: When designing an AI solution, what is "interoperability"?
options: The ability of an AI system to operate only with one vendor ecosystem when planning enterprise AI architecture.
The capability of different AI components or systems to work together and exchange information effectively.
The response speed of an AI model when handling user requests at runtime for reliable production AI delivery.
The process of designing a model's internal algorithmic approach when integrating AI services across platforms.

questions.text: Which of these is a key consideration for a solution architect integrating a third-party AI service?
options: The visual branding and interface style of the selected AI service provider.
Data privacy, security, API documentation, and service reliability.
The personal preferences and working habits of the vendor development team.
The size of the vendor engineering team assigned to the AI service.

questions.text: What is a "design pattern" in software engineering, and why might it be relevant for AI solution design?
options: A specific algorithm used to generate designs or user-interface layouts for reliable production AI delivery.
A reusable solution to commonly occurring problems in software design, which can offer proven AI integration strategies.
A visual diagramming tool for drafting high-level AI model concepts when planning enterprise AI architecture.
A mandatory documentation template applied to every AI project regardless of context during solution design and governance review.

questions.text: When designing an AI application, what is a "data contract" (or data schema agreement) between systems?
options: A legal agreement governing data sharing between two companies when integrating AI services across platforms.
A formal agreement defining the structure, format, and meaning of data exchanged between different components or services.
A security control for encrypting AI data while it moves between systems during solution design and governance review within the same architecture decision context.
A document describing the visual layout of a database or reporting screen for reliable production AI delivery.

questions.text: What is the difference between "model inference" and "model training" from an architectural perspective?
options: Inference always uses GPUs while training always relies on CPUs when planning enterprise AI architecture.
Inference is using a trained model to make predictions; training is the process of teaching the model from data. These often have different hardware/scalability needs.
Inference is used only for small models while training is reserved for large models for reliable production AI delivery.
Inference takes place before deployment while training only happens after release when integrating AI services across platforms within the same architecture decision context.

questions.text: In the context of AI governance design, what is a "model card"?
options: A payment card used to fund AI services and cloud model usage during solution design and governance review.
A short document accompanying an AI model, providing details on its performance, usage, limitations, and ethical considerations.
A physical inventory card showing the deployed model version number when integrating AI services across platforms within the same architecture decision context.
A storage component that holds model weights for later deployment when planning enterprise AI architecture.

questions.text: When designing an AI deployment strategy, what is the role of a "feature store"?
options: A marketplace where teams can publish, purchase, and reuse AI models for reliable production AI delivery.
A centralized system for managing, serving, and sharing engineered features consistently across model training and inference.
A database dedicated to storing raw data before feature engineering when planning enterprise AI architecture.
A reporting tool used to visualise which features influenced predictions during solution design and governance review within the same architecture decision context.

questions.text: What does "container orchestration" (e.g., Kubernetes) provide for deploying AI models?
options: A scoring method for arranging AI-generated music into production tracks when integrating AI services across platforms within the same architecture decision context.
Automation for deploying, scaling, and managing containerized applications (including AI models) across clusters of machines.
A security method for encrypting data stored inside container images during solution design and governance review.
A design tool for building graphical user interfaces around AI services for reliable production AI delivery.

questions.text: In the design of an AI-powered product, what is a "feedback loop"?
options: A recurring software defect that appears repeatedly in the AI application code when planning enterprise AI architecture.
A mechanism to capture user interactions or real-world outcomes to continuously improve the AI model over time.
A communication layer that lets multiple AI models exchange messages for reliable production AI delivery.
A workflow component that causes a model to repeat a task until stopped when integrating AI services across platforms.

questions.text: What is "data lineage" and why is it important in complex AI data architectures?
options: Tracking the organisational history of the developers who built the AI model during solution design and governance review within the same architecture decision context.
Tracing the flow of data from its source through various transformations to its use in AI models, crucial for data governance and debugging.
A performance optimisation method for reducing model training time when integrating AI services across platforms.
A classification method for grouping datasets by type, owner, or domain when planning enterprise AI architecture.

questions.text: Which architectural pattern is often preferred for highly scalable and responsive AI inference services?
options: Nightly batch processing where predictions are produced after daily jobs finish.
Real-time, event-driven microservices with serverless functions or containerized endpoints.
A single monolithic application deployed to one fixed on-premise server when planning enterprise AI architecture.
Manual release and scaling work performed by operations teams on request during solution design and governance review.

questions.text: Why are "adversarial examples" a significant concern for security architects when designing AI systems?
options: They reduce runtime performance by forcing models to process more complex inputs when integrating AI services across platforms within the same architecture decision context.
They are subtly manipulated inputs that cause AI models to make incorrect predictions, posing security risks in critical systems.
They describe AI systems that deliberately become hostile toward end users during solution design and governance review.
They are ordinary training errors caused by incomplete or poor-quality datasets for reliable production AI delivery.

questions.text: When designing an AI system, what is the significance of a "service mesh" like Istio or Linkerd?
options: To manage model weight files during distributed deep learning training when planning enterprise AI architecture.
To provide advanced traffic management, observability, and security features for microservices, including AI endpoints.
To create a dedicated network where AI models communicate directly for reliable production AI delivery.
To package and deploy AI models to edge devices with less overhead when integrating AI services across platforms within the same architecture decision context.

questions.text: In the context of data pipelines for AI, what is a "data lakehouse" architecture?
options: A specialised database intended only for storing AI model artefacts during solution design and governance review.
A hybrid data architecture that combines the flexibility of data lakes with the data management features of data warehouses.
A streaming platform for moving IoT data into analytics systems in real time when integrating AI services across platforms within the same architecture decision context.
A graphical interface for exploring and visualising large datasets when planning enterprise AI architecture.

questions.text: What is "model quantization" in AI deployment?
options: Splitting a large AI model into smaller components for easier deployment for reliable production AI delivery.
A technique to reduce the precision of numerical representations (e.g., from 32-bit to 8-bit floats) in AI models to optimize for inference speed and memory.
A security method for preventing unauthorised access to deployed AI models when planning enterprise AI architecture within the same architecture decision context.
Adding extra layers to a neural network to increase model capacity during solution design and governance review.

questions.text: Which of these is a core responsibility of an Enterprise Architect in driving AI adoption across an organization?
options: Building separate AI models directly for each individual business unit when integrating AI services across platforms within the same architecture decision context.
Defining AI strategy, establishing architectural principles, identifying common platforms, and ensuring alignment with overall enterprise objectives.
Manually cleaning every dataset before it is used in model training during solution design and governance review.
Personally approving and managing every budget for individual AI projects for reliable production AI delivery.
